meta.name: Unstable SVT - Failed Vagal and Adenosine, Synchronized Cardioversion

patient.name: Diana Ramirez-Walsh
patient.chiefComplaint: Sudden onset of rapid heart rate, palpitations, lightheadedness, chest pressure, and feeling like she might pass out

history.hpi: 48-year-old female was sitting at her home office desk when she felt her heart suddenly start racing approximately 30 minutes ago. She describes a 'fluttering, pounding' sensation in her chest. Within a few minutes she became lightheaded, weak, and developed central chest pressure rated 5/10. Her husband noticed she looked pale and was struggling to put words together coherently. She has had two prior similar episodes over the past 2 years - one resolved spontaneously after breath-holding, and one required an ED visit where she was reportedly told she had 'a fast rhythm called SVT' and discharged with a follow-up plan she has not yet kept. No prior cardioversion or ablation. No new medications, no caffeine binge today, no decongestants, no recreational drugs, no recent illness. Denies pregnancy (verified - currently menstruating).
pastMedical: Two prior episodes of 'SVT' over 2 years - one self-terminating, one requiring ED
Mild anxiety - on no daily medication
No known structural heart disease, no prior echo abnormality
medications: No PRN beta-blocker, no calcium channel blocker, no antiarrhythmic
history.lastOralIntake: Coffee at 0700, granola bar at 0930, water through the morning - normal intake
history.events: Sudden onset palpitations 30 minutes ago at her home office desk. Progressive lightheadedness, chest pressure, weakness. Husband called 911 when she said 'I think I'm going to pass out' and looked pale and confused.

scene.dispatch: Respond priority 1 to a private residence for a 48-year-old female with rapid heart rate and chest pain. Patient is awake but reportedly very weak. Husband on scene.
scene.location: Two-story private residence, home office on the main floor. Patient seated upright in a desk chair. Adequate lighting. Comfortable indoor temperature. Direct path to the front door for stretcher.
scene.safety: Scene is safe. Family dog confined. No environmental hazards.
scene.bystanders: Husband (works from home next door office) is at bedside. He has the patient's medication list and recalls her prior SVT diagnosis. He is anxious but cooperative.
visualCues: Middle-aged female slumped in a desk chair, leaning back with her hand on her chest
Visibly pale skin with diaphoresis on her brow
Computer screen still on, calendar event open
Apple watch on her wrist showing a heart rate alert (HR 210)
Half-finished cup of coffee on the desk
Husband holding her hand, watching closely
No signs of trauma or medication overdose
Empty water bottle on desk - not dehydrated

phases.name: Initial Presentation - Borderline Unstable SVT
phases.description: Crew arrives to a 48-year-old female with rapid narrow-complex tachycardia at 210, hypotension (SBP 86), and early hemodynamic compromise (lightheadedness, central chest pressure, mild altered mental status, diaphoresis). Patient meets criteria for 'unstable' tachycardia per the protocol (chest pain, hemodynamic compromise). However, she is still conversant and the team has time for one focused vagal maneuver attempt and a single adenosine push while preparing synchronized cardioversion in parallel.

clinicalPresentation.breathing: RR 24, mildly tachypneic but speaking in short sentences. Lungs clear bilaterally. No accessory muscle use.
clinicalPresentation.circulation: Carotid pulse rapid and regular at ~210. Radial pulse weak and thready. Skin pale, cool, diaphoretic. Capillary refill 3-4 seconds.
clinicalPresentation.pupils: PERRL 4 mm bilaterally - mildly dilated from sympathetic surge.
clinicalPresentation.motorFunction: Symmetric, full strength, no focal deficit.
otherFindings: Patient appears anxious, slightly confused but oriented to self/place/time x3 with effort
Reports 'my heart is racing... can't catch my breath'
Central chest pressure 5/10, non-radiating, no diaphoresis worsening
No JVD, no peripheral edema
No signs of pulmonary edema (lungs clear, no orthopnea)
Apple watch confirms HR ~210 since onset
clinicalPresentation.patientSpeech: 'It's racing... I feel weird... going to pass out... please help me.'  3-5 word phrases.

expectedActions.action: Perform scene size-up, BSI, primary survey; identify regular narrow-complex tachycardia with hemodynamic compromise (chest pain, hypotension, mild AMS) - she is on the unstable end of the spectrum
expectedActions.rationale: Recognizing instability changes the algorithm: failed vagal/adenosine in an unstable patient leads to synchronized cardioversion, not to diltiazem or further rate-control medications.

expectedActions.action: Position the patient supine if tolerated; this also facilitates vagal maneuvers
expectedActions.rationale: Supine improves preload and is the recommended position for modified Valsalva.

expectedActions.action: Apply oxygen via nasal cannula at 4 L/min titrated to SpO2 94-98%
expectedActions.rationale: Diaphoresis and tachycardia warrant supplemental O2; NC at low flow is sufficient since SpO2 95%.

expectedActions.action: Apply 4-lead cardiac monitor and acquire 12-lead ECG; identify regular narrow-complex tachycardia at ~210 (no clear P waves, narrow QRS) - Supraventricular Tachycardia (likely AVNRT)
expectedActions.rationale: 12-lead distinguishes SVT from atrial flutter, atrial fibrillation, or wide-complex tachycardia (VT vs SVT with aberrancy) - each has different management.

expectedActions.action: Establish a large-bore IV (18 g) in the antecubital fossa or as proximal as possible; saline lock - critical because adenosine must be given through a proximal large vein with rapid flush
expectedActions.rationale: Adenosine has a half-life of ~10 seconds; distal small-vein access risks the drug being metabolized before it reaches the heart.

expectedActions.action: Check blood glucose; treat hypoglycemia per protocol if < 70 mg/dL
expectedActions.rationale: Hypoglycemia is a routine work-up element and reversible cause of altered mental status.

expectedActions.action: Coach the patient through a vagal maneuver: modified Valsalva is preferred (15 seconds of forced exhalation against a 10 mL syringe at 40 mmHg, then immediate supine with passive leg raise to 45 degrees for 15 seconds)
expectedActions.rationale: Modified Valsalva increases conversion rates significantly over standard Valsalva. Carotid sinus massage is an alternative but contraindicated in patients > 50 with possible carotid disease.

expectedActions.action: Prepare Adenosine: draw up 6 mg in one syringe and 12 mg in a second syringe; have a 20 mL NS flush in a third syringe attached via a stopcock or have two providers ready for simultaneous push-flush technique
expectedActions.rationale: Adenosine push-flush technique is critical for delivery before metabolism; pre-prep saves seconds.

expectedActions.action: In parallel, prepare for synchronized cardioversion: turn defibrillator on, switch to SYNC mode, set energy at 100 J biphasic (Zoll X-Series for unstable SVT), apply pads in anterior-lateral or AP position, draw up Midazolam 2 mg + Fentanyl 50 mcg for sedation/analgesia
expectedActions.rationale: Parallel preparation - this patient is on the cusp of unstable. If adenosine fails or she deteriorates, you don't want to be hunting for pads and sedation.

transitions.condition: Vagal failed; Adenosine 6 mg IV pushed with 20 mL NS flush, no rhythm change after 1-2 minutes
Crew treats patient as stable SVT and gives diltiazem instead of cardioverting after adenosine fails

phases.name: Vagal and Adenosine Failed - Patient Decompensating
phases.description: Vagal maneuver produced a brief change on the monitor but no conversion. Adenosine 6 mg IV with rapid flush produced a few seconds of asystole on the monitor (expected), then the SVT immediately resumed at the same rate. The crew gave a second dose of Adenosine 12 mg IV - again no sustained conversion. Patient blood pressure has now dropped further (SBP 78) and her mental status is worsening. She is clearly unstable and requires immediate synchronized cardioversion. The pads are already on, sedation/analgesia drawn up, and the device is in SYNC mode at 100 J.
phases.triggerCondition: Vagal failed AND adenosine 6 mg failed AND adenosine 12 mg failed; patient decompensating

clinicalPresentation.circulation: Pulse rapid and now weak even at carotid; radial barely palpable. Skin cooler, more diaphoretic.
otherFindings: Patient mental status declining - longer pauses before responses
Brief asystole observed during adenosine push, then SVT resumed within seconds
Husband visibly alarmed
Crew clearly meets the unstable criteria: hypotensive, altered, chest pain
clinicalPresentation.patientSpeech: 'I'm scared... I feel really bad...' Words coming slower.

expectedActions.action: Verbalize that the patient now meets criteria for unstable tachycardia (hypotension, AMS, chest pain) and that the next step per protocol is synchronized cardioversion - NOT additional rate-control medications
expectedActions.rationale: The protocol bifurcates clearly: stable -> meds, unstable -> sync shock. Continuing with diltiazem or metoprolol in this patient is wrong.

expectedActions.action: Administer Midazolam 2 mg IV slowly over 1-2 minutes for sedation (titrate to dissociation/light sleep, max initial 5 mg in this 70 kg patient); reassess respiratory drive
expectedActions.rationale: Synchronized cardioversion in a conscious patient requires sedation; midazolam 0.025-0.05 mg/kg is a standard dose. Conscious cardioversion without sedation is cruel.

expectedActions.action: Administer Fentanyl 50 mcg IV slowly over 1-2 minutes for analgesia (~0.7 mcg/kg for 70 kg; max initial 100 mcg)
expectedActions.rationale: Pain control complements sedation. Fentanyl has minimal hemodynamic effect, advantageous in a hypotensive patient.

expectedActions.action: Confirm the defibrillator is in SYNCHRONIZED mode (look for marker over each R wave); if SYNC indicator not on, do NOT shock - re-engage SYNC
expectedActions.rationale: Asynchronous shock during the relative refractory period of the T wave can induce VF. Verify SYNC every time.

expectedActions.action: Deliver synchronized cardioversion at 100 J biphasic; ensure crew clear; press and HOLD the shock button until the device delivers (it waits for the next R wave); re-confirm SYNC and re-arm if escalating
expectedActions.rationale: Holding the shock button is required for synchronized delivery (the unit fires on the next R wave). Forgetting to hold is a common error.

expectedActions.action: Immediately after the shock: assess pulse, monitor rhythm, mental status, vital signs; document time and energy delivered
expectedActions.rationale: Determine whether conversion occurred. If SVT persists, escalate energy (150 J then 200 J) per Zoll dose-escalation pattern.

transitions.condition: Synchronized cardioversion at 100 J converts SVT to sinus rhythm
Defibrillator was NOT in sync mode and shock landed on T wave - induced VF

phases.name: Conversion to Sinus Rhythm - Stabilization and Transport
phases.description: Synchronized cardioversion at 100 J converted the SVT to sinus rhythm at 92 bpm. Blood pressure recovers to SBP 118, mental status clears within 1-2 minutes, and the patient reports the chest pressure has resolved. The crew reassesses, leaves pads in place, acquires a post-conversion 12-lead, transports to the closest hospital with cardiac/EP capability, and provides early notification.
phases.triggerCondition: Synchronized cardioversion converted SVT to sinus rhythm with palpable pulse and rising BP

clinicalPresentation.circulation: Strong regular radial pulse at 92, capillary refill 2 seconds.
otherFindings: Patient drowsy from midazolam but easily roused; oriented x3
Chest pressure resolved
Post-conversion 12-lead shows sinus rhythm without ST-segment changes
Husband visibly relieved
clinicalPresentation.patientSpeech: 'It stopped. Wow, that's so much better. I'm a little dopey from the medicine but my chest doesn't hurt.'

expectedActions.action: Acquire a post-conversion 12-lead ECG; look for delta waves (Wolff-Parkinson-White), ST changes, or signs of ischemia from prolonged tachycardia
expectedActions.rationale: Post-conversion ECG can reveal pre-excitation (WPW) which has implications for future medication selection (avoid AV nodal blockers).

expectedActions.action: Leave defibrillator pads in place; maintain monitor; have device immediately available for re-arrest or recurrent SVT
expectedActions.rationale: SVT can recur, especially if there's an accessory pathway; pads stay on.

expectedActions.action: Monitor sedation depth with continuous capnography; the patient may have residual sedation that warrants positioning, oxygen titration, and gentle stimulation if needed
expectedActions.rationale: Standard post-procedural sedation monitoring.

expectedActions.action: Reassess full vitals (HR, BP, RR, SpO2, EtCO2, mental status) every 5 minutes; document trend
expectedActions.rationale: Standard reassessment cadence.

expectedActions.action: Transport to a hospital with cardiology / electrophysiology capability per local destination protocol; provide early notification including initial rhythm, vagal/adenosine attempts, sync cardioversion at 100 J, time of conversion, post-conversion 12-lead
expectedActions.rationale: Definitive treatment may include EP study and ablation; the receiving center should know.

expectedActions.action: If SBP remains < 100 after conversion, administer Normal Saline 250-500 mL IV bolus; reassess
expectedActions.rationale: Some post-conversion patients are hypovolemic from prolonged tachycardia and oral intake decrease.

expectedActions.action: At the ED bedside, structured SBAR handoff including downtime in SVT, vagal maneuver attempts, adenosine doses, cardioversion energy and time, sedation/analgesia given, and current rhythm; provide all monitor strips
expectedActions.rationale: Strip documentation supports the receiving team's evaluation and any EP referral.

phases.name: Branch - Treated as Stable SVT, Continued with Diltiazem
phases.description: After adenosine failed, the crew elected to treat the patient as stable SVT and gave diltiazem. The patient was not stable - she had hypotension, chest pain, and altered mental status. Diltiazem in a hypotensive SVT patient further drops the blood pressure precipitously due to its negative inotropic and vasodilatory effects. The patient is now severely hypotensive and obtunded, approaching peri-arrest. The crew must recognize the error and proceed to immediate synchronized cardioversion with vasopressor support.
phases.triggerCondition: Crew administered diltiazem (or metoprolol) instead of cardioverting an unstable patient

clinicalPresentation.airway: Patent but at risk; reduced gag reflex.
clinicalPresentation.circulation: Carotid weak, radial absent; skin mottled.
otherFindings: Mental status precipitously declined after diltiazem
BP crashed from 78 to 60 systolic
Patient nearly unresponsive
Husband panicked

expectedActions.action: Recognize that diltiazem in an unstable SVT was inappropriate and the patient is now peri-arrest; commit immediately to synchronized cardioversion
expectedActions.rationale: Cognitive correction is needed to break out of the wrong algorithm path.

expectedActions.action: Administer reduced-dose sedation given the deeply hypotensive state: Midazolam 0.5-1 mg IV slowly OR omit if patient is already minimally responsive; preserve hemodynamics
expectedActions.rationale: Standard sedation may further drop BP. In a peri-arrest patient with low GCS, sedation may not be needed at all.

expectedActions.action: Deliver synchronized cardioversion at 100 J biphasic immediately; if SVT persists, escalate to 150 J then 200 J
expectedActions.rationale: Shock is the only definitive therapy for unstable SVT; further delay risks cardiac arrest.

expectedActions.action: Prepare and initiate Epinephrine push-dose 10-20 mcg IV every 2-5 minutes titrated to MAP > 65 to support BP through the cardioversion period
expectedActions.rationale: After diltiazem-induced hypotension, the patient may need pressor support beyond what cardioversion alone provides.

expectedActions.action: Run 500-1000 mL Normal Saline IV wide open through both IVs
expectedActions.rationale: Volume support partially reverses diltiazem hypotension and supports hemodynamics during cardioversion.

phases.name: Branch - Asynchronous Shock Induced VF
phases.description: The defibrillator was inadvertently left in defib mode (not SYNC) when the shock was delivered. The shock landed on the T wave (relative refractory period) and induced ventricular fibrillation. The patient is now in cardiac arrest. The crew must immediately recognize the new rhythm, initiate compressions, and defibrillate (now appropriately in defib mode at maximum energy 200 J) following the cardiac arrest algorithm.
phases.triggerCondition: Shock delivered while defibrillator was NOT in SYNC mode, induced VF

clinicalPresentation.airway: Requires basic airway management; OPA placement.
otherFindings: Iatrogenic VF from asynchronous shock
Pads still in place from cardioversion attempt
Husband in shock, needs to be moved out of the immediate work area
Teaching point: ALWAYS verify SYNC indicator before shocking

expectedActions.action: Recognize induced VF arrest; confirm pulselessness; initiate immediate high-quality compressions at 100-120/min, depth 2-2.4 inches
expectedActions.rationale: Cardiac arrest immediately triggers the arrest algorithm.

expectedActions.action: Switch defibrillator OUT of SYNC mode; deliver immediate 200 J biphasic shock; resume compressions for 2 minutes without pulse check
expectedActions.rationale: VF requires asynchronous defibrillation at maximum manufacturer setting.

expectedActions.action: Administer Epinephrine 1 mg (1:10,000) IV during the first or second cycle; flush with 20 mL NS; may repeat every 3-5 minutes
expectedActions.rationale: Standard ACLS dose for arrest.

expectedActions.action: If VF persists after second shock, give Amiodarone 300 mg IV/IO bolus (may repeat 150 mg in 4-6 minutes)
expectedActions.rationale: Antiarrhythmic for shock-refractory VF/pulseless VT.

expectedActions.action: Manage airway with passive O2 via NRB and OPA for first 3-4 cycles, or BVM at 10/min, or place SGA; do NOT interrupt compressions
expectedActions.rationale: Airway management should not interrupt compressions.

expectedActions.action: Note for debrief: ALWAYS verify the SYNC indicator is illuminated before delivering a synchronized cardioversion; pressing SYNC again after each shock if escalating energy
expectedActions.rationale: Iatrogenic VF from missed sync is a known and preventable error.

criticalActions: Recognize regular narrow-complex tachycardia at 210 with hemodynamic compromise (hypotension, AMS, chest pain) as unstable SVT
Establish proximal large-bore IV (antecubital) for adenosine delivery
Acquire 12-lead ECG to confirm SVT (rule out wide-complex tachycardia)
Perform a vagal maneuver (modified Valsalva preferred)
Administer Adenosine 6 mg IV with rapid 20 mL NS flush; if no conversion, repeat with Adenosine 12 mg IV with rapid flush
Recognize when adenosine fails in an unstable patient: proceed to synchronized cardioversion, NOT diltiazem or metoprolol
Provide procedural sedation (Midazolam 2 mg IV) and analgesia (Fentanyl 50 mcg IV) before synchronized cardioversion when patient is conscious
Verify SYNCHRONIZED mode is active (look for marker over each R wave) before pressing shock
Deliver synchronized cardioversion at 100 J biphasic; press and HOLD the shock button until the device delivers on the next R wave
After conversion: leave pads on, acquire post-conversion 12-lead, monitor for recurrence
Transport to a hospital with cardiology/EP capability and provide early notification
expectedActions: Position the patient supine to support preload and facilitate Valsalva
Confirm there is no pre-excitation (delta waves) on the post-conversion 12-lead, as it changes future medication selection
If SBP remains < 100 after conversion, give a 250-500 mL NS bolus
Document time of onset of SVT, duration, vagal/adenosine attempts, sedation/analgesia given, energy and time of cardioversion, time of conversion to sinus
bonusActions: Use the modified Valsalva (forced expiration against syringe + leg raise) which has higher conversion rates than standard Valsalva
Have a second provider ready for the push-flush adenosine technique to ensure rapid arrival at the heart
Pre-position cardioversion pads while preparing adenosine - parallel preparation
Recognize that pre-excitation (WPW) on post-conversion ECG means future episodes should NOT receive adenosine, calcium channel blockers, or other AV nodal blockers

learningObjectives: Differentiate stable from unstable tachycardia using the unstable criteria: hypotension, altered mental status, chest pain, signs of shock, acute heart failure
Apply the SVT algorithm: vagal -> adenosine 6 mg -> adenosine 12 mg -> if stable, calcium channel blocker or beta-blocker; if unstable, synchronized cardioversion
Use the modified Valsalva maneuver (15 sec forced expiration against syringe + supine with passive leg raise) for higher conversion rates
Administer adenosine via the most proximal IV possible with rapid 20 mL flush due to its 10-second half-life
Verify SYNCHRONIZED mode before every cardioversion and re-engage SYNC after each shock if escalating
Use 100 J biphasic as the typical first-shock energy for unstable SVT (escalate to 150, 200 J as needed)
Provide procedural sedation (midazolam) and analgesia (fentanyl) for any conscious patient receiving synchronized cardioversion
Identify post-conversion ECG findings: pre-excitation (delta waves) implies WPW and changes future therapy
Recognize iatrogenic VF from asynchronous shock as a preventable error of missed SYNC verification
discussionQuestions: What features defined this patient as unstable rather than stable SVT? At what point in the algorithm do those features change your treatment?
Walk through the modified Valsalva. Why does it work better than standard Valsalva?
Why must adenosine be given through a proximal IV with a rapid flush? What happens to the drug if you don't?
What is the difference between SYNC and DEFIB mode on the monitor, and what error can occur if you mix them up?
How do you decide on the energy level for synchronized cardioversion? Does it differ for SVT, atrial fibrillation, and VT?
What is the role of pre-excitation (WPW) in this patient's future care if the post-conversion ECG shows delta waves?
How would you adjust your approach if she had been pulseless or actively seizing on arrival?
commonPitfalls: Giving adenosine through a distal hand IV - drug metabolizes before reaching the heart
Not flushing adenosine rapidly enough or not using a 20 mL flush
Administering diltiazem to an unstable hypotensive patient and watching the BP collapse
Forgetting to verify SYNC mode before shock - induces VF
Forgetting to press and HOLD the shock button (the unit waits for the next R wave)
Performing cardioversion on a conscious patient without sedation/analgesia
Not pre-positioning cardioversion pads while preparing adenosine - delays therapy if adenosine fails
Not acquiring a post-conversion 12-lead - misses pre-excitation diagnosis
keyTakeaways: Stable SVT: vagal, adenosine, then rate control medications (diltiazem, metoprolol)
Unstable SVT: vagal/adenosine optional, sync cardioversion is the answer
Sync cardioversion: 100 J biphasic for SVT, escalate as needed
Always verify SYNC indicator before shocking; press and HOLD
Adenosine needs proximal IV + rapid 20 mL flush + push-and-flush technique
